data.title: ClearBridge International Growth ADR Strategy Q1 2026 Commentary
data.description:
By Elisa Mazen | Michael Feldman, CFA | Michael Testorf, CFA | Pawel Wroblewski, CFA

Resetting as Growth Becomes More Attractive

Market and Performance Overview

International equities declined meaningfully in March as the U.S. and Israel’s military conflict with Iran escalated materially through the month, leading to mixed results for the first quarter after two positive months. The core benchmark MSCI EAFE Index finished down 1.2% yet extended its leadership over the S&P 500 Index (-4.3%). Value stocks outside the U.S. continued to exhibit strength, with the MSCI EAFE Value Index managing a gain of 2.0% for the quarter, outperforming the MSCI EAFE Growth Index (-4.7%) by 670 basis points. Value is ahead of growth by over 1,700 bps for the trailing 12 months.

While international markets had been preparing for a period of lower rates, which would have been beneficial to growth stocks generally, rates began to move higher following the start of the Middle East conflict. Equity markets are still grappling with long-term implications of these moves and, most importantly, their duration.

Exhibit 1: MSCI Growth vs. Value Performance

With continued headwinds from value leadership, especially in energy, the ClearBridge International Growth ADR Strategy underperformed its primary MSCI EAFE benchmark but outperformed its secondary MSCI EAFE Growth benchmark.

The biggest obstacle affecting relative performance was a lack of traditional energy exposure in a three-month period which saw the energy sector soar 40%. Given the selloff in March, higher-beta names in areas such as financials, consumer discretionary and information technology (IT) names were most impacted.

"We are seeking out companies supporting energy security and benefiting from increased fiscal spending in Europe."

The Iran conflict created immediate oil and gas supply disruptions that directly affect an energy-dependent Europe already struggling with the cutoff of Russian energy due to the war in Ukraine. Closure of the Strait of Hormuz also raised the risk of higher inflation from commodity price spikes and caused the European Central Bank to reverse course on what was expected to be a continued program of rate cuts. These rapid and dramatic changes, combined with profit taking after strong performance in 2025, sent the financials sector down 3.7% for the quarter and pressured portfolio holdings Adyen (ADYEY), Intesa Sanpaolo (ISNPY) and NatWest Group (NWG).

Banks, particularly European banks — which represent our largest exposure — saw some profit taking after a standout 2025 and have also served as a source of funding for banks in other regions, insurance companies and stock exchanges, all of which are perceived as more defensive. Notably, Australian and Canadian banks have shown resilience, being less affected by rising oil and gas prices or potential rationing. Over the quarter, we trimmed some of our banking exposure as several holdings approached their target prices.

The launch of Claude Cowork and other generative AI tools in the quarter heightened concerns that AI-native solutions could alter competitive dynamics in IT, increase pricing pressure, extend the sales cycle or compress long-term growth rates. As a result, the software sector — historically viewed as stable and durable due to high recurring revenue and strong retention — experienced broad-based multiple contraction. This dynamic impacted nearly all software companies, including those we believe are relatively well-positioned to benefit from AI or are less susceptible to disruption. We broadly reduced the portfolio’s software exposure in the quarter, including cutting Germany’s SAP (SAP) by roughly two-thirds.

Despite this short-term weakness, we are optimistic about the outlook for international growth stocks and confident that the work we have put into the portfolio in recent quarters positions the Strategy very well for improved outcomes going forward. After an extended period of value outperformance, we believe growth stocks have given up much of their valuation premium.

Exhibit 2: International Growth Premiums Have Compressed

Portfolio Positioning

We saw indiscriminate selling in March across our investable universe, especially among what we consider emerging growth companies with higher revenue growth and innovative business models that carry higher risk. We used the spike in volatility to repurchase shares of video streamer Spotify (SPOT). Prior to the conflict-driven selloff, we exited Shopify (SHOP) and MercadoLibre (MELI) as they reached our price targets.

We are also seeking out companies supporting energy security and benefiting from increased fiscal spending in Europe; these include defense stocks and those enabling electric grid infrastructure such as new buy French utility Engie (ENGIY). Supporting our expectation of higher infrastructure spending in Europe as well was the purchase of U.K.-based global mining company Rio Tinto (RIO), the world’s largest miner by revenue and second largest by profit. While its business has historically been dominated by iron ore, more than half of its future profits will be generated from copper, aluminum, lithium and other industrial metals in high demand.

In addition, we continued to move more meaningfully into Japan with five new positions that reflect our confidence in a number of tailwinds: the country is an outlier in developed markets in that interest rates are rising, which should meaningfully boost net interest margins for banks; ongoing governance reforms are driving higher returns on equity and increasing shareholder-friendly actions such as stock buybacks; and expansionary fiscal policy under the consolidating Takaichi administration that should support domestic demand, defense spending and technology investment.

We purchased ORIX (IX), which provides capital and financing to public and private sector clients and operates its own real estate and renewable energy assets; Pan Pacific International Holdings (DQJCY), an operator of discount retail chains; Shin-Etsu Chemical (SHECY), a supplier of specialty materials to the semiconductor, construction and pharmaceutical sectors; and Sumitomo Electric Industries (SMTOY), a manufacturer of electrical components that power grid connection, transmission and data networking equipment. A portion of these buys came were funded from the sale of European aerospace and defense contractor Airbus (EADSF) which was approaching our price target.

Within IT, we are roughly neutral to the semiconductor capital equipment industry after taking some profits in the strong-performing ASML (ASML). We diversified exposure to this critical picks and shovels area of AI buildouts with the first-quarter additions of ASM International (ASMIY) and Lasertec (LSRCY). ASM is the leader in atomic layer deposition, a precise deposition technique required in the most advanced semiconductors. The Dutch company’s main clients are logic foundries Taiwan Semiconductor (TSM), Samsung (SSNLF) and Intel (INTC), as well as memory foundries SK Hynix and Micron Technology (MU). Foundries and wafer fab equipment providers like the ASM work closely, which gives the firm insight into the customer's innovation and product road maps. Japan’s Lasertec is a pure-play leader in inspection and measurement equipment that is well-positioned to benefit from the emerging growth drivers of extreme ultraviolet (EUV) memory adoption and AI infrastructure. We took advantage of an attractive entry point given that the company is at trough earnings with order recovery ahead.

The sales of Japanese IT consultant Nomura Research Institute (NRILY) and U.K. business information provider RELX (RELX) were driven in part by negative sentiment over AI disintermediation risks that are currently overwhelming the fundamentals of these businesses. We exited Israeli security software maker Check Point (CHKP) because our thesis on revenue growth acceleration is taking longer to play out than expected.

Outlook

War has changed cost economics around the world, which we know is not good for Europe due to its energy dependence, as we saw during the early stages of the Russia-Ukraine war. How long this lasts and what the result will be has yet to be determined, especially with Iran controlling the Strait of Hormuz, and we must look at potential impacts across several areas. We are analyzing how inflationary pressures from higher commodity prices could impact consumers, as well as what they mean for input prices for industrial and materials companies.

We have also been carefully vetting the portfolio to be better in tune with the current risk environment and ensure we are appropriately positioned for the next leg of growth. We have reduced our IT overweight while continuing to add to utilities and materials. We see these structural companies as the new growth stocks and have seen early signs validating our thesis. We have also been moving more meaningfully into Japan, and to a lesser extent Australia, highlighting an overall priority to add in places where we see inexpensive valuations and rising earnings.

While the Strategy has very limited exposure to oil and gas stocks — which acted as a major performance headwind in the quarter — we are also looking at our energy exposure, achieved through owning utilities and select industrials, as a way to play defense and be well-positioned to participate in a period of surging power demand. This contributed to our decision to add a position in Australian LNG company Woodside Energy during the quarter.

Portfolio Highlights

During the first quarter, the ClearBridge International Growth ADR Strategy underperformed its MSCI EAFE Index benchmark but outperformed its MSCI EAFE Growth Index secondary benchmark. On an absolute basis, the Strategy produced positive contributions across three of the 11 sectors in which it was invested: industrials, materials and utilities. The consumer discretionary and financials sectors were the chief detractors.

We have been monitoring on a regular basis and reporting to our clients on the performance of the MSCI EAFE (core), MSCI EAFE Value and MSCI EAFE Growth indexes in order to provide context on market movements for growth investors.

Relative to the MSCI EAFE Index, overall stock selection and sector allocation detracted from performance. In particular, stock selection in consumer discretionary, financials, communication services and IT, an underweight to energy and an overweight to consumer discretionary weighed on results. On the positive side, stock selection in the industrials and health care sectors proved beneficial.

Relative to the MSCI EAFE Growth Index, overall sector allocation contributed to performance. In particular, overweights to materials and utilities, an underweight to IT and stock selection in industrials and health care supported results. Conversely, stock selection in consumer discretionary, financials, consumer staples and IT and an underweight to industrials hurt performance.

On an individual stock basis, the largest contributors to returns relative to the MSCI EAFE Index in the quarter were ASML in IT, Siemens Energy and BAE Systems in industrials, Agnico Eagle Mines in materials, and AstraZeneca in health care. The greatest relative detractors were Sea Limited in consumer discretionary, Nomura Research and SAP in IT, Tencent in communication services as well as Adyen in financials.

In addition to the transactions mentioned above, we repurchased shares in Deutsche Boerse in financials. The Strategy also closed positions in Argenx in health care, Celestica in IT and Ferrari in consumer discretionary.

Elisa Mazen, Managing Director, Head of Global Growth, Portfolio Manager

Michael Feldman, CFA, Director, Portfolio Manager

Michael Testorf, CFA, Managing Director, Portfolio Manager

Pawel Wroblewski, CFA, Managing Director, Portfolio Manager

data.title: Niagen Bioscience: How Its Shift To Telehealth Makes It A Strong Buy
data.description:
The market treats Niagen Bioscience (NAGE) as a traditional nutritional supplement company. However, it is undergoing a strategic transformation to become a leader in telehealth and longevity as a service. This sector offers exceptional profit margins and higher valuations compared to the traditional supplement industry.

The main catalyst that will drive the stock in the long term is management's plan to launch Niagen Plus injectables through its telemedicine platform in the first half of 2026 by taking advantage of the change in consumers' behavior brought about by GLP-1 weight loss drugs.

I believe the same consumers who overcame the barrier of self-injection to maintain their weight would be ready to do the same to maintain their youth. This shift should change the company's unit economics and increase the customer's long-term value, justifying re-rating the stock at HealthTech multiples. I rate the stock a Strong Buy at a price of around $6-$8 per share, representing a potential upside of 40% to 75%.

Competitive Advantage

Niagen is building a comprehensive system for Niagen Plus products to be available in 1,200 health clinics in the United States. However, what will change the game is the launch of subcutaneous injections with smaller doses of 100 milligrams at a cheaper price—less than half of in-clinic IV sessions.

This shift will reshape the company's revenue stream, as in typical e-commerce models, the cost of acquiring a customer is high compared to the purchase price, but in telehealth, the customer is obligated to pay a subscription to get the injections, which generates recurring revenue that significantly reduces the customer acquisition cost (CAC). Additionally, the direct sales to consumers through its platform should eliminate the intermediaries and distributors, ensuring that the majority of revenues are recorded as gross profit.

In addition, the company has a set of patents and clinical research that protect it against competitors, especially the sellers of NMN (nicotinamide mononucleotide), as the FDA's reversal of its previous decision regarding banning NMN as a dietary supplement opens the door for many companies to sell NMN at competitive prices. The management didn't remain idle; it cited a study published in the Cell Journal last January, including a comparison between Niagen and NMN. The result showed that taking Niagen led to more than double the levels of the NAD+ enzyme compared to NMN. To further strengthen its moat, NAGE announced a strategic acquisition last December of a patent portfolio from Queen's University Belfast covering the chemical formulations of NR salts, which gives the company the required flexibility to develop more stable and effective formulations for Niagen Plus injections, securing exclusive rights for the advanced forms of NAD+ boosters.

Financials & Outlook

During 2025, the company achieved net sales of $129.4 million with excellent growth of 30% compared to last year. This growth was primarily driven by its Tru Niagen brand, which grew by 27% to reach $97.7 million. The direct e-commerce sales also jumped to $74.1 million. On the other hand, the ingredients sector recorded tremendous growth of 45%, or $27.9 million. However, it's worth noting that pharmaceutical-grade Niagen ingredients achieved staggering growth of 123% to reach $3.8 million, which reflects a significant improvement in sales after the launch compared to 2024.

However, Q4 specifically showed a deceleration in growth rates to 16% compared to previous quarters, which exceeded the 30% barrier, but it explains the conservative approach from the management when presenting 2026 guidance.

Net income reached $17.4 million with a growth of 103%. Although this figure looks impressive, it includes non-operational elements, as the company recorded an accounting gain of $2 million due to a settlement of intellectual property fees. In addition, it recovered $1.3 million from a bad debt provision related to a legal settlement with Elysium Health company. However, this doesn't reduce the company's performance, as if we excluded these exceptional gains of $3.3 million, the core earnings would still be positive and confirm that the company has crossed the break-even point and started a sustainable profitability phase.

Management expects a revenue growth between 10% and 15% YoY. While this figure seems disappointing for an investor who is used to 30% growth, the CFO stated that the revenues will be focused in the second half, indicating that the management isn't basing its expectations on just the continued organic growth of Niagen capsules, but the year will also see the launch of their revolutionary product, Niagen Plus Home Injections. Therefore, I think the management chose to give easily achievable expectations through their core business while leaving the door open for huge surprises in the second half when the revenues of the telemedicine platform start to flow in.

Value Per Share

NAGE trades at around $4.5 with outstanding shares of 80.08 million, implying the company’s market cap is $359.5 million. Given the company has cash of $65 million, the EV is approximately $294.5 million. Considering the net income is $17.4 million in 2025, the stock trades at a trailing P/E of 22x and a forward P/E of 19x. While this multiple seems fair or a little high for a nutritional supplement company, it makes sense since the company will shift to a subscription model once it launches its telemedicine platform. Therefore, when comparing it with other HealthTech peers such as Hims & Hers (HIMS), which trades at a historical P/E exceeding 39x and a forward P/E & EV/Sales approximately the same as NAGE, we realize that the market started to give the company valuations like the telehealth companies. NAGE still has significant room for expansion and is protected by IP, as it holds exclusive commercial rights on its products, unlike HIMS, which has fierce competition in selling generics. I'm assuming Niagen will continue to improve operating leverage. By applying a premium forward P/E of 25x justified by its IP moat or taking into account $150 million in sales in 2026 with EV/Sales of 3x, which is the historical average, we will have an EV of about $450 million and an equity value of $515 million/$6-$8 per share, representing potential upside of 40% to 75%.

Risks To Consider

Niagen Bioscience heavily relies on W.R. Grace & Co.-Conn. as an exclusive and sole supplier of NRC material, as there is a contract that obliges W.R. Grace to be the exclusive manufacturer for NAGE until April 30, 2029. The problem is that W.R. Grace has a patent for the crystal structure of the material, which makes it almost impossible for NAGE to find an alternative in a short time. Therefore, if any dispute occurs between them or a disruption in the supply chains, this could lead to a complete paralysis in the company's sales.

According to section B503 of the FD&C Act, the NRC substance is included in the temporary list of bulk substances permitted for use in compounding formulations, as the company cooperates with FDA-registered facilities to distribute Niagen Plus. If the company fails to meet regulatory standards or the FDA changes its position on keeping the substance on the permissible list, this could close this segment, and thus our growth thesis for Niagen Plus could fail.

Final Thoughts

I believe that people's interest in maintaining their youth equals or even surpasses their interest in losing weight, as the ultimate goal is to be in good shape. The company seems to be in a good position thanks to the shift in consumer behavior regarding self-injections and its acquisition of QUB's patent portfolio to compound advanced NAD+ boosters. However, this depends on how effective its products are and how well people accept them. We should keep an eye on the launch schedule of Niagen Plus, which is supposed to be in H1 2026, and the potential market adoption of it. I believe any sign indicating a decrease in customers' acquisition cost or high retention rates will push the market to reprice the company as a health tech company and spark the stock to rally.

data.title: Very Bad News For Altria: This Dividend King Is Running Out Of Runway
data.description:
Back in 2023, I last covered Altria Group (MO), and at the time I rated it a hold, as its high yield and Dividend King status indicated that it had a lot going for it, but at the same time I had concerns about its underlying business model.

Since then, the stock has actually performed very well, delivering about 89% over that period of time, which outpaced the S&P 500. While I was on the sidelines due to my neutral outlook on the company and have therefore missed out on the strong total return performance, in today's article, I'm going to re-look at the investment thesis to determine whether or not it is worth buying today.

Still a Dividend King - But For How Long?

MO remains one of the most beloved dividend stocks in America, as its 56-year dividend growth streak puts it firmly in the dividend king category while also boasting a dividend yield of over 6%, making it a high-yielding stock. That being said, its strong recent performance means that its valuation is not as cheap as it once was. Is this strong recent stock price performance validated by improvements in the underlying business model?

The Marlboro Moat is Cracking

Well, MO does have a dominant position in the cigarette space, as its Marlboro brand holds about 40% of the U.S. cigarette retail market share, which is way above the competition. This business brings in the vast majority of its income, as its smokable product segment generates between 80–90% of its total operating income. However, this business has not really grown its revenue much at all in quite some time because it has had to increase its prices fairly aggressively in order to offset its steep volume declines.

Its customer base is overwhelmingly older generations that are gradually dying off, while younger demographics are not engaging in cigarette-smoking behavior at the same rate that previous generations did. In fact, domestic cigarette volumes have declined by 9.5% in 2025. The U.S. overall smoking rate has fallen from a whopping 42.4% in 1965 to just 11.6% by 2022, and the trend continues to point towards even lower smoking rates. On top of that, Marlboro's total retail share is also declining. It stood at a commanding 50% in 2018 and has declined to about 45% by the end of 2025, with its retail share dropping to below 40% in Q4 2025 for the first time ever.

Squeezed From Both Ends

Part of the reason for this is that the company has leaned heavily on price increases to sustain its profit growth, while inflationary pressures are squeezing the low-income consumers, which has pushed them to adopt deeply discounted cigarettes more aggressively. MO has been using a basic brand to try to help retain these customers, but it is still reducing its profit margins. Thus, MO is now being squeezed on both fronts, both from declining overall demand for the product itself as well as increasing pricing pressures.

A Costly History of Diversification Failures

The company is also seeing its oral tobacco segment stumble, as its retail share fell from 36.1% to 29.6% year over year in Q4 this past year. When you combine this weakness with the fact that MO has struggled with diversification efforts in the past, as its $12.8 billion JUUL investment was a massive loss for shareholder value, the $1.8 billion Cronos cannabis investment also appears to have destroyed shareholder value, and even its own nicotine pouches, despite their growth, remain a small segment that they are not able to come close to putting a dent into the decline in the cigarette business. Even the NJOY acquisition that they sunk $2.75 billion into ultimately led to a $1.3 billion non-cash impairment charge in Q4 2025.

This gives me great caution that MO will be able to diversify effectively away from its cigarette business. Meanwhile, though they have outperformed my expectations in sustaining profits due to price increases in their cigarette business, this too seems to be nearing the end of its runway due to pricing pressures and continued volume declines.

Balance Sheet & Growth Outlook: Red Flags Ahead

All this will likely put significant pressure on the company's ability to sustain its strong balance sheet and grow its dividend at a strong clip moving forward. The balance sheet still contains substantial debt from the JUUL purchase, with total debt to EBITDA at 2.0 times. The good news is that these debt maturities are well laddered through 2038, with no single year exceeding $2 billion in maturities. The company's $10.3 billion Anheuser-Busch InBev (BUD) stake is performing fairly well and delivers nice dividends to its cash flow statement.

Still, at a forward price-to-earnings ratio in the low teens, which is near the high end of its recent valuation range and its increasingly challenging growth outlook, it is hard to make the case for MO here. In fact, analyst consensus estimates only see revenue growing at a 0.6% CAGR through 2030, EBITDA growing at a 0.9% CAGR over that same period, and earnings per share growing at a 2.7% CAGR over that time.

Why the 6% Yield Isn't Enough to Buy This Shrinking Ice Cube

Thus, we are looking at what will likely be at best inflation-level growth, and quite possibly less than that, meaning that MO's shrinking ice cube in terms of volumes is likely at an inflection point of being about to show up in its bottom line as well, as net of inflation, MO is likely going to be a shrinking ice cube in the near future. While that does not break the investment thesis entirely, a dividend yield of a bit over 6% is not enough to entice me to adopt that sort of valuation outlook, especially given that its next 12-month price-to-earnings ratio of 11.7 times is well above its three-year average of 9.8 times.

This means that valuation multiple expansion is unlikely to be a material tailwind moving forward. Therefore, I simply do not see a clear path to double-digit annualized total returns moving forward, and that MO is likely to deliver mid-single-digit annualized total returns moving forward.

Overall, while MO does have a wide moat in its core business, that moat is showing some cracks, and the growth story appears to be running out of runway. Therefore, MO is likely going to be at best a bond proxy in the coming years, which puts it firmly into the hold category for me. While the company has been able to outrun my previous pessimism by posting strong returns in recent years, it is increasingly difficult for me to see a bullish scenario for it in the future. Therefore, I remain on the sidelines and am pursuing other high-yielding, high-quality dividend growth opportunities in my 7–8% yielding total return outperforming and below-market beta portfolios at High Yield Investor.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Intel: Turnaround Is Real, But The Valuations Went Bonkers (Upgrade)
data.description:
Intel Proved Me So Wrong!

The case of Intel Corporation (INTC) is one that offers me a timely reminder why things could change quickly in the semiconductor space.

With INTC moving quickly to retake highs that we last saw in April 2021, it has even surpassed my wildest imagination when I decided to downgrade Intel stock in January.

I acknowledged Intel's turnaround, but I didn't give enough thought to the possibility that the market wants to take it even higher.

At this point, the valuations have also reached a stage where I find it conflating, but the market doesn't really bother about what I think, anyway.

INTC's price action has never looked better over the past few months. When compared to its semiconductor peers (SMH), I believe you could see the robust uptrending print that defines the optimism poured out into the stock.

It's getting clear that the market is now convinced that there is a fundamental shift in Intel's recovery. One that investors appear very keen to keep pounding the table on.

Intel: The Tide Has Turned To CPUs

The fact remains that Intel is still the market share leader in data center CPU, with >60% share. Sure, that gap with rivals has narrowed considerably over the years, putting pressure on its core business.

Yet, with the Agentic AI era now firmly establishing the importance of the CPU, Intel's opportunity to capitalize has surely arrived.

Arm's (ARM) decision to enter the CPU space is a timely one. As CEO Rene Haas opined, the "rise of AI Agents" is pivotal to the current phenomenon underscoring the return of the core business in CPUs.

While I don't think it has invalidated Nvidia's (NVDA) AI chips business, it has also refreshed the competitive moat for Intel's stronghold in the CPU slate, reminding us that they are far from being made obsolete.

And what’s so exciting is that these signals aren’t even theoretical to begin with.

They are already being felt, as recent signals show “CPU usage is accelerating again to support AI training and inference beyond head nodes.”

We are looking at an inflection point for CPU gains here.

And, one in which I believe will place Intel firmly in the catbird seat to capture these highly beneficial economics as it looks to regain the bullish narrative from undecided investors like me, right?

It’s the age of the CPU.

This makes sense why the market went bonkers on Intel stock. That ties in nicely with all the Claude Code fever going on right now.

And Anthropic (ANTHRO) is reportedly or rumored to be short of compute capacity.

OpenAI (OPENAI) thinks that Anthropic’s relative lack of ambition in stuffing itself with more compute has now left it strategically disadvantaged.

Could it now create the opening for OpenAI to regain control of the agentic conversation, and wrestle it away from Anthropic’s control?

Likewise for Intel.

But, Has The Market Gone Way Overboard?

INTC has undoubtedly outperformed Nvidia (NVDA) over the past year. But, when you consider the respective free cash flow margins, they aren’t even close.

Why is the market willing to give so much benefit of the doubt to Intel right here, right now? It's baffling to say the least.

At least for Anthropic, its annualized revenue run rate crossed $30B, as compared to the $25B that OpenAI reportedly garnered.

However, the fact is that the market is looking forward, right?

And that means that the runway for Intel to regain control of the narrative has arguably improved by leaps and bounds, and that’s just for the products side of the house.

And the forward EPS growth outlook for Intel is now in tune with Nvidia over the next two years (>50% average growth), although one could say that INTC’s forward earnings multiple of 125x has such a distinct disconnect when compared to NVDA’s 23.7x.

Like, seriously! What am I not getting right?

Both companies are expected to grow at roughly the same rates, but only one has managed >200% return in the past year.

Something must be missing in the jigsaw puzzle that I haven’t quite solved. What could it be?

Ah ha... I guess you could say Intel Foundry.

Intel Foundry Now Front And Center

I guess even Nvidia investors are confused. The promises baked into that very high INTC multiple contrast sharply with the entrenched dominance led by Nvidia’s systems, but accorded a much lower multiple.

Foundry is unquestionably the biggest optionality for Intel, yeah?

And this twin pillar of growth, CPUs and chip production, is now front and center.

If you want to find an American chipmaker that is integrated on the product front, chipmaking, and advanced packaging, Intel is really front and center.

The tie up with Elon Musk (TSLA) (SPACE) on Terafab is still in the very early stages.

I’m not even sure when we will even get a tape out, much less think about risk production. Does anyone have any idea?

If anything, a five-year period before ramping isn’t unreasonable, right?

And then, we have to think about whether Musk is merely relying on Intel’s capabilities in chipmaking before he vertically integrates that in-house.

I bet he’s thinking about doing most things in-house, especially for chipmaking. But that’s still a massive undertaking, and thus, Intel should still play a primary role for the foreseeable future.

And buying back Fab 34 from Apollo Global (APO) obviously lends credence to the foundry story, demonstrating the resolve and eagerness of Intel in getting back to business in foundry, while also “[reinjecting] operating leverage” back to its foundry business.

These steps create a credible long-term bull case for Intel’s chipmaking capabilities, and could send them back to the pantheon of chipmaking greats that they used to be, right?

If Elon Musk doesn’t think TSMC (TSM) can give him enough production capacity while the rest of the customers also compete for wafer allocations, then Intel has a really solid opening this time.

The only thing is, we still need to see firm commitments on 18A, even though the market’s enthusiasm suggests that they are probably waiting for some announcements already.

Can the advanced packaging discussions reportedly with Amazon (AMZN), and Google (GOOGL) translate into notable deals and be good enough to convince the market that anything short of 18A commitments or orders is good enough?

If they do, these might offer Intel more than just hope when thinking about having possible customers for 18A, even though I don’t think management sees it as the base case.

For now, the strategy is still to try and fill external commitments for 14A, but the market might have already gone above and beyond looking for possibilities for 18A orders.

Nevertheless, 18A-P will still represent a very encouraging start, and I think the market is definitely placing hopeful bets on it actualizing.

Intel management is clearly trying to calibrate its penetration into the foundry space amid extremely tight advanced packaging capacity for the agentic era.

And Intel believes that even if someone else’s wafers are used, the company can still do the advanced packaging business, and one that is supposedly more efficient.

So, that opening is real, and something that the market is really trying to price in. Although, I think Wall Street still needs more conviction before it will adjust the earnings growth to reflect already well-elevated multiples.

If these announcements don’t come soon enough, I fear that such high multiples don’t quite have the execution to justify them, right?

Buy Or Sell INTC Stock?

Nonetheless, INTC’s chart is one that really fascinates me right now.

Take a closer look.

April 2021 was when INTC peaked and then entered a multi-year downward spiral that finally culminated in a bottom at around the $20 level.

That was April 2025. A four year breakdown, which I guess probably eviscerated many of the bulls who lost hope with INTC’s thesis.

One year on from those “hopeless” days, Intel has rediscovered its mojo, outperforming semiconductor peers since then. Could it still go higher?

Incidentally, Intel stock has also inched back toward the $70 level.

That level previously defined where sellers came back and walloped the stock in 2021, disallowing it from going anywhere higher in the next four years.

Five years on, Intel stock appears to have been reinvigorated, and the bad memories appear to have been (nearly) fully extinguished from the print.

No longer chasing after AI GPUs that aren’t really its forte, as the market is now moving back toward CPUs in the Agentic AI surge, which is Intel’s core business.

Struggling to keep up with TSMC’s tech no longer seems to be such a structural disadvantage any longer, as the world mulls not even having enough production capacity as the bigger downside, especially in advanced packaging.

Intel’s fortunes have taken such a dramatic turnaround that it has also surprised me thoroughly, as it flipped my narrative on its head.

The trend bias favoring INTC over the semi peers indicates that this is a multi-month recovery that may pan out to be a multi-year one.

However, with valuations now having moved well ahead of execution, it will now be up to CEO Lip-Bu Tan and his team to show me whether the story is justified.

So for me to go fully bullish right here, right now, does seem a tad too late for me, as the market came back with such a vengeance to invalidate my previous rating.

That said, a bearish rating is now looking stale and no longer tenable to validate.

Persisting with it would have needed me to go against the dominant trend, which is against my nature of how price action (and the market) works.

Rating: Upgrade to Hold.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: M&T Bank: Robust Performance In Q1
data.description:
In today’s column, we continue our coverage of the regional bank earnings season by checking in on M&T Bank (MTB). As one of the larger and more sophisticated regional players in our coverage universe, M&T Bank often provides the first real look at how the commercial and industrial sectors are weathering the current interest rate environment and recent economic concerns stemming from the Iran War. Of course, the stock has seen some interesting price action leading up to this report, having faced some pressure alongside the broader financial sector over the last month. It has recovered here some in April with the market rebound. After digging through the just-reported Q1 results, we see the bank as continuing to be resilient with disciplined capital management. Despite the broader macroeconomic concerns, M&T Bank managed to deliver a report that exceeded expectations on several fronts. We think the bank stock is a buy. In this column, we will break down the key metrics and discuss what this means for investors. Let us discuss.

Headline results

M&T Bank reported headline results that significantly outpaced what analysts were looking for. The bank reported EPS of $4.13, up nicely from the $3.38 reported in the same quarter last year. On an adjusted basis, the beat was even more pronounced, coming in at $4.18, well above the consensus estimate of $4.01. This was driven by a nice top line move. Total revenue was $2.44 billion, up 6% year-over-year. This revenue growth was primarily fueled by a 3% rise in net interest income and a very strong 13% increase in non-interest income. Solid results overall here. It is impressive to see this kind of top-line growth at a time when many regional peers are struggling to maintain momentum, though it is early in our coverage of Q1 earnings season.

Margins expanded

One of the most critical indicators we follow for the banking sector is the net interest margin, and M&T Bank did not disappoint here. A good number of regional banks are seeing a flattening yield curve that compresses their margins, though funding costs have improved some with the cost of deposits coming down. M&T Bank actually saw its net interest margin expand. The margin came in at 3.71% in Q1, a sequential 2 basis point improvement from the 3.69% in Q4 and a 5 basis point move up from the 3.66% seen a year ago. The bank's asset yields are currently rising faster than its funding costs, which is exactly what you want to see in this part of the cycle. Management’s ability to price loans effectively while managing deposit costs going forward is something we will monitor. It is also worth noting that the bank’s outlook for 2026 net interest income remains solid, with guidance calling for $7.2 billion to $7.35 billion for 2026.

Loans and deposits

Loans and deposits are the engines of any regional bank, and the trends here offer a unique look at the economic health of the Northeast and Mid-Atlantic corridors where M&T Bank operates. On the lending side, average total loans grew to $138.4 billion, which was a 3% increase from last year and a 1% sequential increase. The growth was led by commercial and industrial lending, as well as residential real estate and consumer loans. This suggests that business owners and consumers in the bank's core markets remain active and confident enough to continue seeking credit. Interestingly, we did see a decline in commercial real estate balances. This is something we have seen across the industry as banks intentionally manage their exposure to certain sectors that may be facing higher scrutiny.

Average deposits ended the quarter at $164.3 billion, a slight dip from the $165.1 billion reported in the sequential quarter, but they remain higher than the prior year levels. We are seeing a general stabilization in deposits across the regional space, and while there is always some competition for rate-sensitive funds, M&T Bank’s deposits have held mostly firm of late

Asset quality

Now, we must look at asset quality, which is where we often find the first signs of trouble if the economy is starting to soften. M&T Bank’s credit metrics remain very resilient. Net charge-offs for the quarter were 0.31% of average loans, which is actually a slight improvement from the previous year. Non-accrual loans also declined to 0.89% of total loans. These are very healthy numbers. However, we did note that the provision for credit losses was $140 million, a bit higher than the $125 million seen in the prior quarter. This increase in the provision is likely more of a cautionary move or a reflection of loan growth rather than a sign of actual credit stress. The allowance for credit losses was maintained at 1.53% of total loans. In our view, the bank is well-prepared for any potential downturn, and the current credit profile does not raise any red flags.

Shareholder-friendly

We also want to point out that as a bank, it has dividends each quarter, which is a nice bonus, and the company is buying back stock. During the first quarter alone, the bank repurchased $1.25 billion of its common stock. This is a massive amount of capital being returned to investors, and it shows the high level of confidence management has in the bank’s future.

Looking ahead

Looking ahead, the bank is performing at a very high level. Management has issued solid guidance for the remainder of 2026, and while we will continue to monitor the broader economy for signs of weakness, M&T Bank is positioned well. The combination of margin expansion, disciplined loan growth, and significant shareholder returns justifies a buy.

data.title: Workday: A Welcome Reset For The Long-Term Investor (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
Introduction

Since the last time I covered Workday, Inc. (WDAY), the company’s share price tumbled 45%, and now that we have the full-year performance out, I thought it would be a good time to revisit it. This massive reset in valuations, I think, was overdone, and I believe that there is a good opportunity for the long-term investor to invest in Workday at these levels.

The Performance

Let’s take a look at the overall top-line performance over the last year, now that we have all the information needed. In general, we can see that the company’s revenues have been trending in the right direction, with no meaningful slowdown that I can see. A 13.1% y/y increase overall is respectable. Subscription revenues were up 14.5% to $8.833B, while professional services declined slightly to $719m.

Overall, the company saw revenue growth accelerating in the last quarter of the year, which is the opposite of some other companies that I covered in the past, which experienced a massive decline in their share price. The growth was very steady, and then it increased, while the share price declined by over 49% for the year.

In terms of profitability, I can see that margins across the board improved quite a bit as well. Gross margins remained steady, while the largest improvement can be seen in the company’s EBIT and EBITDA margins. Both are improving by around 460 bps. That is quite a profitability boost. The management attributed the overall improvements to ongoing efficiencies, like slower headcount growth and process/AI-driven cost optimizations.

An even more impressive metric that SA doesn’t capture is the company’s non-GAAP operating margin, which came in at around 30.5% for the year. I thought the EBITDA margin would be close to that, but it seems that the company does a lot more adjustments in general to get to such a number. Share-based compensation accounted for 15% of the increase, which EBITDA doesn’t take into account.

Let’s take a look at the company’s financial position. WDAY finished the year with around $1.5B in cash and equivalents, as well as $3.9B in marketable securities. That is a good amount of liquidity on hand against $3B in long-term debt. This is not an issue at all. The liquidity can easily cover all the debt outstanding, but why bother when the capital can be focused on continuing to expand operationally and gain a higher market share from its competitors? I would argue a good way to use the cash right now is to do some share buybacks since it is down significantly from its highs and now trading at a forward P/E ratio of just over 11x. Additionally, the company being asset-light, its CapEx is negligible, which means most of the cash from operations turns into free cash flow. This time around, free cash flow came in at around $2.78B, an increase of $2.2B from last year. The company continues to be a cash-generating machine.

Overall, the performance has been quite good; the top-line growth, instead of slowing down, actually increased, the company has become a lot more profitable, and its balance sheet is very strong. So, why the disconnect? Why has its share price plummeted 49% over the last year, while its operations continued to improve?

Sentiment Shift

Being a SaaS company means most likely that the share price was going to get hit quite a bit due to the whole SaaSpocalypse. Investors were becoming very jittery as they saw that what was happening with AI could affect SaaS companies dramatically. They seem to believe that AI will replace the need for companies like WDAY with the help of AI agents. Enterprises will start building their own autonomous agents, so the fear is that AI is going to disrupt the SaaS model and weaken the pricing power that these companies have, and from what it looks like, they expect companies like WDAY to just disappear overnight, if we look at what happened to its share price. A 50% drop in value is not to be taken lightly. It says that investors are expecting a fundamental shift in the company’s business for the worse.

To me, it all sounds like it is a bit of an overreaction. Sure, the guidance that the company provided for the next quarter and the year fell slightly below expectations, but that hardly warranted such a reset in expectations. I think what the investors disliked the most was the fact that the FY27 subscriptions guide implied a deceleration compared to FY26’s 14.5% growth. Expectations were through the roof for many of these SaaS companies, so such a reset is quite healthy but also likely overdone, which could present an opportunity.

Not The End Of Companies Like Workday

I also believe that this bearish narrative of WKDAY and the rest becoming obsolete because every enterprise will be able to create their own HR platform for cheaper forgets the fact that not every company even wants to attempt doing that. They will have to weigh the risks. What if, after spending billions trying to recreate what WDAY already has achieved and is good at, it ends up being a far inferior platform? Why try something that potentially has a chance of saving some money when you can just use a company that has deep expertise and knows its way around the ever-changing dynamics of the current technology and advanced technology like AI? WDAY will continue to have decent performance because they are actively improving their offerings with the most advanced tech that everyone is looking to employ, like AI agents. On top of that, the company has deep integration into payroll, GL, compliance, audit, security, and data governance. That is a lot to ask for if an enterprise wants to make something similar from scratch. Some research suggests that enterprises will be looking to embed task-specific AI agents in 2026, but a lot of that integration is expected to be through focusing on picking the right platforms and vendors and not necessarily building everything in-house.

That is exactly where WDAY will get its next growth phase, or at least maintain the current pace, in my view. The company is pushing hard into AI agents. The company is building role-based AI agents for HR, finance, and many corporate functions, rather than just a simple task bot, with Workday Assistant and Sana at the forefront, which are orchestrating these AI workers across the workflows. The company launched Agent System of Record, which is marketed as a central place to govern, monitor, and control AI agents, whether they were made by them or third-party ones, alongside human workers.

So, the narrative that WDAY is going to be dead because AI agents are taking over seems false; WDAY is the one building AI agents, which will keep customers attached to its ecosystem in the long run. Why would they build something if there is a very good product out there already for them?

Valuation

With such an improvement in profitability and a plummet in share price, I am interested to see what the company might be worth currently. Below you will see the assumptions I used for the company’s operations. They are anchored close to what analysts are expecting.

I went with a flat 10% CAGR for the model because some analysts expect higher and some lower. For operating margin, I am using the company’s non-GAAP numbers, as that is what everyone seems to be focusing on. For the rest, for simplicity, I went with the company’s average historical numbers. For my margin of safety, instead of using the company’s WACC of around 8% as my discount rate, I went with 10%, which should be more than enough of a margin. Also, I am going with 2.5% as my terminal growth rate, as usual. With these assumptions, we get the following projections.

These numbers seem more than achievable; the growth in earnings isn’t particularly wild and is in line with the past growth. And with these projections, we see that Workday’s intrinsic value is around $174 per share, which means the sentiment drove down the premium significantly, and the company is trading at a decent discount.

Risks And Closing Comments

Look out for further slowdown in growth, which seems to be what many analysts are focusing on right now. If it drops off completely, then I can see a lot more pain ahead in terms of share price depreciation. The dynamics of growth will have shifted, and many investors who were still holding on to a recovery will jump ship.

AI is a disruptor, and it evolves very quickly, so if the company drops the ball, it will lose momentum and market share to a more nimble competitor who will swoop in at better prices and better technology overall.

The fatigue in the AI growth space might continue to push any stock related to AI down as the bubble continues to expand, and with further uncertainty in the macroeconomy, AI names will get hit the most, since this is essentially the only growth story out there.

I’ve covered Workday many times in the past, and I never gave it an upgrade to a buy until now. It was always too expensive for me. The growth was never justified. Right now, with this massive correction, I am upgrading it to a buy, purely because the sentiment is too negative and because the company’s fundamentals have improved significantly over time.

data.title: Spotify: I See The Stock As A Top Hit Ahead Of Q1
data.description:
In my view, Spotify (SPOT) is the go-to music streaming service, and I believe the "go-to" moat is what makes the company attractive at current levels.

The company will be releasing its Q1 towards the end of April, and I will be looking for an encore performance to its Q4 release.

When that was released in early February, the company reported strong user growth and better-than-expected earnings. This helped send shares up about 15% following the release.

With the stock trading at the lower half of its 52-week range and still down about 10% YTD, I believe investors are afforded a reasonable entry point in a market leader, which I believe will remain dominant in the months and years ahead.

SPOT Stock Key Metrics

SPOT isn’t a discounted stock by any means. Shares are graded poorly on valuation by Seeking Alpha’s (“SA”) quant scores, and the multiples do rightly seem elevated.

Its current forward earnings multiple, for example, stands at over 30x. In addition, its enterprise value hovers in the mid-20x to mid-30x range of both EBITDA and EBIT. Bargain hunters are likely not seeing basement bin pricing here.

However, I believe the premium pricing is very much warranted. The company is reporting double-digit percentage sales growth and is crushing its earnings estimates.

In its Q4 release, for example, SPOT beat its earnings estimates by a whopping €1.65/share. I also believe SPOT faces very little competition from other streaming alternatives, such as Apple (AAPL) Music and Amazon’s (AMZN) streaming service.

Sentiment across the analyst community is similarly bullish, with both the SA community and Wall Street highly bullish on SPOT’s prospects. And among Wall Street, consensus targets see over 20% upside potential remaining in the stock.

I very much believe shares can reach this target, especially with continued user growth and earnings strength.

What Is The Outlook For SPOT Stock?

When drilling into the Q4 transcript, I circled the excerpt where founder Daniel Ek framed SPOT as a company that wins at the intersection of consumers and creators.

To me, that’s an important statement to unpack. Here’s the way I see it. Features like Discover Weekly, Wrapped, Spotify for Artists, and the expanding free tier aren’t just product tweaks. They are, instead, proof that SPOT continues to deepen the flywheel between listeners, artists, and advertisers. That ecosystem is only getting stronger, in my view.

What also stood out to me is how aggressively SPOT is leaning into AI and personalization. The team appears to clearly see SPOT as a technology platform for audio, not just a music app.

Tools like Interactive DJ, Prompted Playlists, and advanced mixing features appear to be driving more engagement, more time spent, and ultimately higher lifetime value per user. I see that as a powerful setup for both subscriber growth and monetization.

Financially, I believe the story is lining up with the product vision. Revenue grew 13% in Q4, operating income soared more than 50% for the full year, and free cash flow hit a record €2.9 billion.

With pricing increases sticking, churn staying low, and margins expected to expand further in 2026, I believe that SPOT looks positioned for continued earnings strength alongside its user growth story.

What To Watch When SPOT Reports Q1 Results?

When SPOT reports Q1, I’ll be watching the user numbers first, because that’s ultimately the core of everything that SPOT does.

During Q4, SPOT guided to 759M MAUs and 293 million subscribers. This implies 8 million MAU net adds and 3 million subscriber net adds in what is seasonally the slowest quarter of the year.

In my view, if SPOT can meet or exceed these targets, it would support the idea that user growth here is durable, even after recent price increases.

I’ll also be paying very close attention to ARPU, which is expected to grow 5%–6%. That’s important because it would show that pricing power is sticking without driving meaningful churn. SPOT already noted that churn has behaved in line with expectations, which is exactly what you, as a business, would want to hear after raising prices.

Margins are another key watch item. Q1 gross margin is guided at 32.8%, and the company expects margins to improve through 2026 as revenue growth outpaces content costs. If we see early signs of that playing out, it strengthens the earnings growth narrative, in my view.

Finally, any commentary around AI-driven features like Interactive DJ and Prompted Playlists, and how they are impacting engagement and retention, will be crucial.

Is SPOT Stock A Buy, Sell, Or Hold?

In my view, SPOT is a "Buy" ahead of Q1.

Yes, the valuation looks rich on the surface. A forward earnings multiple north of 30x and EV multiples in the 20x–30x range are not going to attract deep value investors. But I don’t think SPOT should be looked at through a bargain-bin lens.

In my view, this is a company that is growing users at scale, expanding margins, generating record free cash flow, and increasingly proving it has real pricing power.

More importantly, I believe SPOT’s moat is widening. It’s not just the largest music streaming platform. In my view, it’s becoming the default platform where audio content is discovered, shared, and monetized.

Furthermore, the ecosystem between listeners, creators, and advertisers appears to be getting tighter, and the company’s aggressive push into AI-driven personalization is making the product even stickier, in my view.

When you pair that with double-digit revenue growth, operating income up more than 50% last year, and expectations for further margin expansion in 2026, the premium valuation starts to make a lot more sense.

With the stock still trading near the lower half of its 52-week range and consensus targets pointing to roughly 20% upside, I see this as an attractive entry point into a category leader that I believe still has plenty of runway ahead.

data.title: With Warsh Confirmation Pending Focus Reverts To Shrinking Fed Balance Sheet
data.description:
Warsh Confirmation Pending

Fed Chair Nominee Kevin Warsh is supposed to assume his new position when current Fed Chair Jay Powell’s term expires on May 15th, one month from today.

There’s a small matter that needs to take place first, though, and that is Warsh is required to be confirmed by the full Senate.

There are two parts to the confirmation. First, Warsh must be confirmed for a seat on the Board of Governors, which comes with a 14-year term, and separately, he must be confirmed as Chair, which is for a 4-year period.

Procedurally, Warsh must file the necessary Office of Government Ethics paperwork with the Senate Banking Committee, clearing the way for a confirmation hearing before the Committee. These documents include a financial disclosure form and an ethics agreement.

That step was completed on Monday.

With that box checked, the hearing date was set for next Tuesday, April 21st.

The hearing is public, where Senators will question the nominee on critical issues such as his qualifications and his views on interest rates, inflation, the role of the Fed, and Fed independence.

Warsh’s Views On The Fed

We have a precursor to Warsh’s thinking from an Opinion Article he published in the Wall Street Journal in November, while he was in the middle of the interview process for the Fed Chair position.

In “The Federal Reserve’s Broken Leadership” Warsh highlighted four key points:

  1. AI will be a significant disinflationary force in the coming years.
  2. The Fed’s bloated balance sheet can be reduced significantly.
  3. The Fed’s regulatory failures led to the banking crisis of 2023.
  4. The Fed has wasted 10 years trying to work on global bank regulations, known as the Basel Accords, while they should be focusing on what works for the U.S. banking industry and letting the world follow us.

It is clear Warsh wants to take the Fed in a new direction.

The Fed’s Bloated Balance Sheet

Of the issues Warsh highlighted, the Fed’s bloated balance sheet has gotten the most attention recently.

This is a topic Warsh is quite familiar with, as he was previously a Fed Governor from 2006-2011, during which time we experienced the Global Financial Crisis (GFC) of 2008. The Fed was very aggressive in their efforts to prevent the economy from collapsing. They initially used their most effective monetary policy tool by cutting the fed funds rate to almost zero. Once that tool was no longer available to them, they turned to more unconventional methods.

Using Open Market Operations, the Fed embarked on a series of Large-Scale Asset Purchases, more commonly known as Quantitative Easing (QE), to try to stimulate the economy and add liquidity to the system. This resulted in a massive expansion of their balance sheet.

QE was only intended as a stopgap measure, and once the economy stabilized, it was to be unwound. In 2011, after the crisis had passed, Warsh chose to resign from the Fed because he opposed the continuation of QE. Warsh emphasized that relying on low-interest rates risked inflating asset prices and encouraged leverage. He felt QE “involves the misallocations of capital in the economy and the misallocation of responsibility in our government.”

He believed efforts to strengthen the economy should be conducted through fiscal policy, not monetary policy.

The Fed balance sheet totaled $800 billion when Warsh first joined the Fed and grew to $2.5 trillion when he resigned.

As can be seen in the chart above, the Fed continued with QE, then initiated QE again during the pandemic. Altogether, the Fed implemented QE over a 14-year period, and the balance sheet peaked at almost $9 trillion.

Balance Sheet Reduction

The Fed finally implemented their Policy Normalization plan in 2022 when they began raising the fed funds rate and started reducing their balance sheet through maturity runoffs, more commonly known as Quantitative Tightening (QT.)

For 3 ½ years the Fed executed QT, shrinking the balance sheet by $2.4 trillion, a 27% decline. Then, in December 2025, the Fed determined that they had reached their desired level of ample reserves, and they ended QT.

Nominee Warsh thinks there is more room to go.

Research Supporting Balance Sheet Reduction

Within the past month there have been three major studies explaining how the Fed can reduce their balance sheet.

The first is “The Payment System Puts a Floor on the Fed’s Balance Sheet” by Darrell Duffie, a Professor of Finance at Stanford Business School.

The next is “A User’s Guide to Reducing the Federal Reserve’s Balance Sheet” by Federal Reserve Governor Stephen Miran.

And the final paper is “Options for reducing the size of the Fed’s balance sheet” by Dallas Fed President Lorie Logan.

There are many similarities among the papers. Each focuses on the liability side of the balance sheet, and explains ways to get banks to reduce their demand for bank reserves,

These methods include some regulatory changes, a tiering of the interest rates the Fed pays on reserves, and making the Fed’s liquidity tools more accessible, so banks don’t need to hold liquidity continuously.

Looking at the liability side makes sense because the Fed expanded their balance sheet to add liquidity to the financial system by creating bank reserves, so shrinking the balance sheet requires reducing the bank reserves.

However, I see a fundamental problem in ignoring the asset side of the balance sheet.

How To Deal With The Fed’s Unrealized Losses

Liquidity in the form of bank reserves is what the Fed created during QE, and liquidity is what the Fed sought to drain by reducing reserves under QT.

But the mechanism the Fed employed to create and drain reserves is through trading assets, or in this case securities held in the Fed’s System Open Market Account (SOMA.) The Fed bought U.S Treasury securities and Mortgage-Backed Securities during QE, and they comprise 99% of the Fed’s total assets.

In order to reduce their liabilities, the Fed must also reduce their assets, and this is where the problem creeps up.

During QE the Fed deviated from their historical practice of owning only short-term Treasury securities, and they bought longer maturity Treasuries and MBS. Most of these SOMA holdings were purchased when interest rates were much lower. As a result, their current market value is significantly below their cost.

In footnote 5 d in the Fed’s 2025 Combined Financial Statements they explain they are carrying a $535.3 billion unrealized loss in their Treasury holdings and a $309 billion unrealized loss on their MBS holdings. The total unrealized loss of $844 billion represents a 13% haircut from the purchase price of the securities.

The quickest way to shrink the balance sheet is to sell securities, but to do so would require the Fed to record the loss on the holdings.

That is something they have refused to do to date, and are unlikely to do in the future. The Fed has already dealt with large operating losses over the past three years, which cumulatively total $243 billion and are carried in a Deferred Asset account on the balance sheet.

Consequently, the only way to reduce their assets is through security runoffs, which is how Fed has handled QT so far.

This is an important question for the Senate Banking Committee to ask Warsh, how he plans to deal with SOMA losses if the Fed shrinks their balance sheet.

The Fed has consistently stated that their realized and unrealized losses do not affect monetary policy implementation or effectiveness.

But this is not the complete story.

In the Miran study, he addresses Fed losses. In fact, he explains that they are a reason for wanting to reduce the Fed balance sheet. He states “while a central bank can, in theory, operate with an infinitely negative capital position, those losses may impugn the Fed’s credibility and competence in the public mind and thereby threaten independence and scope for effective action. They also induce volatility in the remittance of operating profits to the Treasury since these profits are delayed until the Fed recoups the losses. Volatility in profit remittance induces additional volatility in fiscal deficits, which has its own set of consequences.”

Miran’s study is the first time a Fed official has suggested that Fed losses could be a problem and they validate the criticisms I have written about for more than three years in highlighting this Fed deficiency.

If, in fact, Warsh chooses to reduce the Fed’s balance sheet, it will be a slow process.

Balance Sheet Reduction is Tightening Policy

It should be remembered that shrinking the Fed’s balance sheet by draining reserves is a tightening of monetary policy. It is called Quantitative Tightening for a reason.

Warsh is also going to have to square this during his hearing with his purported aim of reducing rates. President Trump has stated many times that his litmus test for a Fed Chair would be someone who agreed with him about lowering rates. He said “If I think somebody’s going to keep rates where they are…I’m not going to put them in.”

Warsh will have to explain how he can reduce rates, which is an easing of policy, at the same time he is shrinking the balance sheet, which is a tightening of policy. It is like driving with one foot on the gas and one foot on the brake.

Potential Obstacles

Following the hearing, the Committee then votes on whether or not to move the nominee for a vote before the full Senate.

This is where things get tricky.

The Senate Banking Committee is comprised of 13 Republicans and 11 Democrats. A simple majority moves the nomination forward. Presently, Senator Thom Tillis, R-NC, has vowed to block any nominee until the Department of Justice investigation into current Chair Powell is closed.

Tillis has publicly stated “If there were any remaining doubt whether advisers within the Trump Administration were actively pushing to end the independence of the Federal Reserve, there should now be none. It is now the independence and credibility of the Department of Justice that are in question. I will oppose the confirmation of any nominee for the Fed – including the upcoming Fed Chair vacancy – until this legal matter is resolved.”

Despite U.S. District Judge James Boasberg deciding, twice, to block subpoenas issued by the DOJ for documents from the Federal Reserve and Chair Powell, citing there was no evidence of a crime, seemingly ending their criminal investigation, U.S Attorney Jeanine Pirro has indicated that the inquiry will continue.

Senator Tim Scott, who announced the that the hearing date has been set, stated that he thought the DOJ would finish up its investigation within weeks, which would allow Senator Tillis to vote in favor of Warsh. Scott did acknowledge, however, that he has no inside information on DOJ’s plan.

Senator Tillis has reaffirmed that he will not vote for any Fed nominee until the DOJ case against the Chair Powell has ended.

To complicate the confirmation process more, President Trump today said the investigation into the Fed renovation will continue.

All of these obstacles are pushing up against the clock to get Warsh confirmed. From Tuesday’s hearing, there are only 3 ½ weeks until May 15th for the Senate to confirm Warsh. The Senate is out of session for one of those weeks.

Something will have to give in order for Warsh to get the approval of the Banking Committee to be up for approval in front of the entire Senate.

If it does not happen by May 15th, Powell has stated that he will continue to serve as Chair pro tem until confirmation.

Stay tuned.

data.title: The Davenport Funds Q1 2026 Stock Market Update
data.description:
Q1 2026 Stock Market Update

Market Returns (%) Q1 2026 2025
U.S. Large Caps -4.33 17.88
U.S. Mid Caps 1.29 10.60
U.S. Small Caps 0.89 12.81
International Developed Markets -1.24 31.22
Emerging Markets -0.17 33.57
Intermediate Term Bonds -0.02 6.97

Source: Morningstar Direct. Please see last page for index definitions.

Topsy-Turvy

It's hard to write a letter these days. Any attempt to prepare thoughts in advance risks quickly being obsolesced (we hope this letter is still relevant when you read it!). Topsy-turvy is the best way to describe the first quarter of 2026. The year started on a decent note with gains in January. However, renewed tariff concerns, waning enthusiasm for AI-related stocks and war in Iran ultimately became headwinds. The S&P 500 ® Index finished the quarter down 4.33% while the Russell 2000 ® Index finished up 0.89% (having been up 9.54% at one point).

We initially witnessed a rotation within the market. Technology stocks and names linked to artificial intelligence (AI), which were so popular for much of last year, began to see declines. In fact, technology was one of the worst performing sectors this quarter (down 9%). Two potential risks that we discussed at year end started to manifest themselves. Number one was the potential for AI to fuel disruption among the large technology incumbents. This was on full display in Q1, with the various AI participants forming a circular firing squad of sorts. Many prior “winners” became “losers” for fear of disruption and/or loss of leadership status. This disruption narrative was most acute in the software space. The second risk we previously highlighted was the potential for investors to question the massive capital expenditures going into computing power for AI. Indeed, investors started to wince as tech behemoths dramatically increased spending plans while having uncertain paybacks.

Meanwhile, investors started to look for value elsewhere. Cyclical, value-oriented and small cap stocks broadly outperformed for a bit. Interestingly, the equal-weighted S&P 500 meaningfully outperformed the cap-weighted index, which is dominated by large cap tech bellweathers including the so-called “Magnificent 7” 1 . As the tech sector was declining, sectors such as energy, materials and industrials started the year on a strong note as investors began to discount an improving economic environment alongside fiscal stimulus and the potential for further interest rate cuts. We also think there was an element of investors gravitating towards businesses that were deemed more immune from the AI-disruption narrative. Ironically, it started to pay to be outside of the AI fray despite the promise of the new technology.

Then, conflict in Iran took over the headlines. The Strait of Hormuz effectively closed (20-25% of seaborne oil passes through the Strait), oil prices spiked and market dynamics changed as investors began to fear an economic slowdown. While energy stocks gained due to higher commodity prices, we shifted from a market rotation to a market where almost everything was going down. Technology continued to struggle, but consumer, cyclical and interest rate-sensitive stocks were also hit hard. The economic risks of war in Iran are fairly straightforward. For one, higher oil prices mean higher gasoline prices (up $0.75-$1.00 per gallon since the war commenced), which could weigh on consumers' wallets. Higher oil also means upward pressure on overall inflation, which in turn puts upward pressure on interest rates and financing costs. Relatedly, it also decreases the odds of monetary stimulus in the form of Fed rate cuts. Finally, many companies that have a presence in the Middle East are dealing with disruption to their businesses and/or supply chains.

Where now? We were looking forward to the potential benefits of both fiscal and monetary stimulus. The waters are now murkier and higher gasoline prices, should they persist, will offset much of the benefit of Trump's Big Beautiful Bill. However, the President seems very sensitive to both economic conditions and markets, especially with mid-term elections approaching. We aren't sure how easily this conflict can be resolved and the Middle East has always been a quagmire, but any semblance of a resolution, including re-opening the Strait, could prompt a relief rally (we got a taste of this on the last day of the quarter). Remember, we were thrown a Trump curveball this time last year in the form of Liberation Day and tariffs. Uncertainty initially weighed on investor sentiment, but it ultimately paid to lean into fear and be buyers of stocks as worst case outcomes never came to fruition.

Speaking of fear, the CNN Fear & Greed Index resides at 14 out of 100 as of this writing, indicating "extreme fear" on the part of investors. What's more, valuations have become more reasonable with the S&P 500 trading at 19.3x forward earnings and the equal-weighted S&P at 15.6x. Assuming a reasonably swift resolution to Iran (or at least a going from a boil to a simmer), we think many stocks now offer compelling deals. A volatile political environment has brought many surprises and made our job a little more difficult, but we do indeed see value in certain areas. We continue to focus on avoiding disruption at the hands of AI and remain hopeful that economic improvement can continue, thereby benefiting cyclical and consumer-oriented businesses. Thank you for your partnership and please refer to our portfolio letters for a discussion of themes and ideas.

Sincerely,

George L. Smith III, CFA®

Chairman, Investment Policy Committee

1 The Magnificent Seven (“Mag 7”), stocks are a group of high-performing and influential companies in the U.S. stock market: Alphabet, Amazon, Apple, Meta Platforms, Microsoft, NVIDIA, and Tesla
